objections.objection: The scenario assumes admission-equity rules can be designed and enforced consistently across state, church, and foundation maintainers, but the expert record documents maintainer plurality, partial autonomy frameworks for church/foundation maintainers, and an explicit coalition commitment (Tisza's 12‑point programme) to preserve those maintainers' prior operating conditions — all of which make uniform enforceability implausible without addressing legal and political blockers. The claim that uniform national mandates are feasible is therefore unsupported and internally inconsistent with the scenario's own political_risks and uncertainties fields.
objections.suggested_revision: Replace the blanket enforcement assumption with (a) a mandated legal mapping of maintainer autonomy clauses and likely litigation risks; (b) a two-track design that (i) specifies a state-sector mandatory model and (ii) a negotiated incentive/compensation package for church/foundation maintainers (e.g., funding conditionality, negotiated MOUs, purchase-of-service contracts) plus an explicit contingency if negotiations fail (state-only pilots or phased scope-limited rollout); and (c) a plan to pursue entrenchment (cardinal law or cross‑party pact) or a clearly specified ministerial-decree-plus-monitoring fallback, with pilot evaluation metrics to test real-world compliance before national rollout.

objections.objection: The scenario treats anti‑sorting rules for school choice and catchment as a credible co‑requirement that will prevent sorting from migrating to other channels. This is optimistic and contradicted by cited evidence: Finland saw post‑reform erosion via school choice, and Kertesi & Kezdi document migration of sorting even where choice regulations existed. The scenario therefore rests on an unproven, low‑confidence assumption that legal rules alone will block adaptive sorting behavior.
objections.suggested_revision: Recast the design to treat anti‑sorting rules as an experimental, high‑risk complement rather than a guaranteed fix: (1) require a detailed legal feasibility and enforcement-cost assessment for specific anti‑sorting instruments (catchment tightening, admission caps, transfer restrictions, sanctions); (2) build a legally enforceable package (statutory sanctions, funding penalties, license/authorization conditionality) and model likely evasion pathways; (3) mandate regional pilot(s) with pre‑specified migration‑detection metrics and automatic contingency actions (e.g., rolling back phase‑in, targeted compensatory investments) if sorting is observed to reappear in non-gate channels; and (4) include an explicit political durability strategy (cross‑party entrenchment, independent monitoring, phased public communication plan).

objections.objection: S2 assumes state‑sector score thresholds or capacity caps can shrink selective provision without being offset by church/foundation or private providers, but the expert record shows church/foundation gimnázium share grew from ~10.4% to ~27% (2010–2021) and the current coalition commits to preserving church operating conditions — making offsetting expansion a likely outcome rather than a manageable exception. The scenario omits modeling of this leakage and therefore overstates the effectiveness of state‑level capacity contraction as a national instrument.
objections.suggested_revision: Revise to incorporate explicit leakage modeling and concrete countermeasures: (a) run admission‑flow simulations that model probable shifting of displaced applicants to non‑state and private providers under several elasticities; (b) specify legal/incentive levers available to government (e.g., funding conditionality, building‑transfer restrictions, licensing limits) and assess their political/legal feasibility via a short legal review; (c) if legal constraints block direct restriction, redesign the policy to focus on state‑sector contraction paired with mandated compensatory funding for general schools and a public transparency regime tracking non‑state expansion (with pre‑agreed triggers for mitigation measures); (d) commit to piloting in regions where non‑state expansion capacity is limited and evaluate displacement before scaling.

objections.objection: S4 assumes TEIP‑style targeted funding commitments will be durable, yet the scenario's own political_risks and the synthesis cite fiscal consolidation pressure (projected 6.2% deficit, rising debt) and identify targeted funding as a first-line cut — an internal contradiction that makes the durability assumption unsupported.
objections.suggested_revision: Make funding durability explicit and conditional: (1) propose a concrete fiscal mechanism to ring‑fence TEIP funding (e.g., statutory funding floor/cardinal‑law protection, or an indexed multi‑year appropriation tied to objective metrics); (2) include an alternative, lower‑cost prioritized menu (start with grade‑retention reduction measures that have documented net savings and reallocate a portion of those savings into TEIP pilots); and (3) require an early-costing and fiscal-risk statement from the Ministry of Finance before committing to national rollout, with contingency scaling rules if projected consolidation requires cuts.
